title: Beyond N-Grams: Rethinking Evaluation Metrics and Strategies for Multilingual Abstractive Summarization

problem_definition.task: Systematically assess the reliability of automatic evaluation metrics for multilingual abstractive summarization by correlating metric scores with human judgments across diverse languages.
problem_definition.input: Automatically generated summaries and gold reference summaries across eight languages.
problem_definition.output: Pearson correlation coefficients between automatic metric scores and human Likert ratings, stratified by language typology and resource level.
problem_definition.evaluation_target: Demonstrate the limitations of n-gram-based metrics for fusional languages, show the improvements from tokenization adaptations and neural metrics, and advocate for neural metrics specifically trained for evaluation tasks like COMET.

core_method.name: Multilingual Metric Evaluation Protocol
core_method.one_line: A large-scale human evaluation of GPT-3.5-Turbo and Gemini-generated summaries across eight languages from four typological families, collecting ~20,000 human annotations to correlate n-gram and neural metric scores with human judgments.

atomic_steps.description: Select 8 languages from 4 typological families (Isolating: Chinese, Yoruba; Agglutinative: Japanese, Turkish; Low Fusional: Arabic, Spanish; High Fusional: Hebrew, Ukrainian) and group by resource level (High/Low) based on GPT-3 pre-training data proportions.
Sample 400 test articles and reference summaries per language from XL-Sum (for 7 languages) and HeSum (for Hebrew).
Generate system summaries for all 400 samples per language using GPT-3.5-Turbo-0125 and Gemini-1.0-Pro, yielding two summaries per article.

Corrupt a subset of generated summaries using language-specific strategies: for coherence, replace verbs with lemmas, replace conjunctions, or reorder non-adjacent sentences; for completeness, shuffle named entities with the same label and insert an irrelevant sentence from another article.

Design the MTurk annotation task: each HIT displays an article, a qualification question, and two system summaries (one from each model); annotators rate each summary on coherence and completeness using a 1–4 Likert scale, with descriptions of each quality criterion.
Recruit crowd workers via Amazon Mechanical Turk, with 3 annotators per summary pair, and collect ~20,000 total annotations (400 samples × 3 annotators × 2 models per language).
Aggregate human ratings per summary (e.g., mean of the 3 annotators) to obtain final Likert scores for coherence and completeness.

Compute n-gram metric scores for all system summaries against gold references: ROUGE-1/2/3/L with default space tokenization, BLEU, CHRF, ROUGE variants with mBERT tokenizer and with language‑specific monolingual tokenizer, and BLEU computed on lemmatized text for fusional languages.

Compute neural metric scores: MoverScore (with mBERT embeddings), BERTScore (using mBERT and language‑specific BERT models per language), Gemini-as-a-judge (Gemini 1.0 Pro with same prompt as annotators), and COMET (using wmt22‑comet‑da, excluding source input to adapt for summarization).
Calculate Pearson correlation coefficient between each metric’s scores and the aggregated human Likert ratings, separately for coherence and completeness.

Group correlations by typological family: Isolating (Chinese, Yoruba), Agglutinative (Japanese, Turkish), Low Fusional (Arabic, Spanish), High Fusional (Hebrew, Ukrainian); compute per‑family averages.
Group correlations by resource level: High (Chinese, Japanese, Arabic, Spanish) and Low (Yoruba, Turkish, Hebrew, Ukrainian) based on GPT-3 token proportions; compute per‑resource averages.
Report Pearson r values with statistical significance markers (*p<0.05, **p<0.01).

settings.name: full correlation analysis
settings.description: Evaluation of 21 automatic metrics (n-gram and neural, including tokenizer-adapted variants) using 400 samples per language, 3 annotators per summary, Likert-scale ratings for coherence and completeness, with Pearson correlation and p-value significance testing.

datasets.name: XL-Sum
datasets.used_for: source articles and reference summaries for summarization evaluation (Chinese, Yoruba, Japanese, Turkish, Arabic, Spanish, Ukrainian)

datasets.name: HeSum
datasets.used_for: source articles and reference summaries for Hebrew summarization evaluation

metrics.name: Pearson correlation
metrics.definition: Pearson correlation coefficient between automatic metric scores and human Likert-scale ratings for summary quality (coherence and completeness).

main_experiments.claim: Elo score trends from human evaluation: Gemini-generated summaries rank higher for high-fusional and low-resource languages, while GPT summaries rank higher for high-resource languages.

Standard n-gram metrics show negative or weak correlations with human judgments for fusional languages (e.g., ROUGE-L -0.23 for high-fusional coherence), while neural metrics like COMET consistently achieve stronger correlations across all typological families (e.g., 0.35 high-fusional coherence).
COMET yields the highest correlation with human judgments across resource levels; monolingual BERTScore outperforms multilingual BERTScore for high-resource languages and low-resource completeness.
Per-language Pearson correlations for coherence confirm the negative trends in fusional languages (Hebrew, Arabic) and the benefits of adapted tokenization/neural metrics.
Per-language completeness correlations mirror the typological patterns and show that lemmatized BLEU improves high-fusional correlations dramatically (e.g., from -0.10 to 0.40).

expected_claims.claim_text: Standard ROUGE-L yields negative Pearson correlation for high-fusional languages: -0.23 for coherence and -0.18 for completeness.
expected_claims.verification_hint: Check row 5 (ROUGE-L) under coherence and completeness columns for High Fusional; both should be negative as stated.

expected_claims.claim_text: Using the mBERT tokenizer with ROUGE-L changes the correlation for high-fusional languages from negative to positive: -0.23 → 0.08 (coherence) and -0.18 → 0.12 (completeness).
expected_claims.verification_hint: Compare row 5 (ROUGE-L) and row 11 (ROUGE-L mBERT) for High Fusional; verify the sign change and exact values.

expected_claims.claim_text: COMET achieves the highest correlation among all metrics across typological families, with 0.35 for high-fusional coherence and 0.24 for completeness.
expected_claims.verification_hint: Check row 21 (COMET) across all family columns; confirm 0.35 is the highest in high-fusional coherence and 0.24 in completeness.

expected_claims.claim_text: Applying BLEU on lemmatized text improves correlation for high-fusional completeness from -0.10 to 0.40.
expected_claims.verification_hint: Compare row 6 (BLEU) and row 16 (BLEU Lemmatized) under completeness High Fusional; verify the increase to 0.40.

expected_claims.claim_text: Monolingual BERTScore outperforms multilingual BERTScore for high-resource languages (coherence 0.27 vs 0.23) and low-resource completeness (0.21 vs 0.15).
expected_claims.verification_hint: Check BERTScore (mBERT) and BERTScore (Monolingual) rows for High resource coherence and Low resource completeness; verify the stated values.

reproducibility_meta.estimated_compute: CPU only, minutes for metric computation and correlation analysis; crowdsourcing annotation time (multiple days)

figures.caption: Participant Interface in a closed mode: The interface includes three drop-down sections: Instructions, Qualification and the Annotation task.
The Participant Instructions Interface: The participant has general steps and a detailed explanation and examples of each tested criteria.

The Participant Qualification Interface: The human summary is displayed at the top (the example is in Arabic), while the Qualification section below requires the participant to fill in their home state and answer a question generated by Gemini based on the human summary, designed to assess basic comprehension of the provided summary.

The Participant Annotation Interface: Two summaries are displayed side by side. Each criterion includes a slider ranging from 1 to 4, along with an info hover feature providing a reminder of the criterion’s definition.
